coredata.dc:title: Polymer dots grafted TiO2 nanohybrids as high performance visible light photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Gen
Wang, Feng
Liu, Peng
Chen, Zheming
Lei, Ping
Xu, Zhongshan
Li, Zengxi
Ding, Yanfen
Zhang, Shimin
Yang, Mingshu
coredata.dc:description: As a new member of carbon dots (CDs), Polymer dots (PDs) prepared by hydrothermal treatment of polymers, usually consist of the carbon core and the connected partially degraded polymer chains. This type of CDs might possess aqueous solubility, non-toxicity, excellent stability against photo-bleaching and high visible light activity. In this research, PDs were prepared by a moderate hydrothermal treatment of polyvinyl alcohol, and PDs grafted TiO2 (PDs-TiO2) nanohybrids with TiOC bonds were prepared by a facile in-situ hydrothermal treatment of PDs and Ti (SO4)2. Under visible light irradiation, the PDs-TiO2 demonstrate excellent photocatalytic activity for methyl orange degradation, and the photocatalytic rate constant of PDs-TiO2 is 3.6 and 9.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and commercial P25, respectively. In addition, the PDs-TiO2 exhibit good recycle stability under UV–Vis light irradiation. The interfacial TiOC bonds and the π-conjugated structures in PDs-TiO2 can act as the pathways to quickly transfer the excited electrons between PDs and TiO2, therefore contribute to the excellent photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
Polymer dots
Visible light
Photocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relationship between the structure and photocatalytic activity of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 51403217 and 21504003 ]; the "Strategic Priority Research Program" of the Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. XDA09030200 ] and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS [Grant No. 2017041 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photograph of PDs solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (B) TEM image of PDs. (C) HRTEM image of PDs.
TEM images of TiO2 (A, B) and PDs-TiO2-10 (C, D).
(A) FTIR spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10. (B) Ti2p XPS high resolution spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10.

(A) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 (insets: photographs of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 powders). (B) Plots of transformed Kubelka-Munk function versus the energy of absorbed light.

(A) Photocatalytic degradation of MO solution under visible light irradiation (>420 nm). (B) Photocatalytic degradation of MO solution under UV–Vis light irradiation (220–800 nm). (C) Cycle photocatalytic performance test of PDs-TiO2-10 under UV–Vis light irradiation. (D) Photocatalytic rate constant k of P25, pure TiO2 and PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids under visible light irradiation.
(A) EIS changes of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10. (B) PL spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 excitated by UV light (365 nm).

head.ce:title: Polymer dots grafted TiO2 nanohybrids as high performance visible light photocatalysts

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Science
CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new member of carbon dots (CDs), Polymer dots (PDs) prepared by hydrothermal treatment of polymers, usually consist of the carbon core and the connected partially degraded polymer chains. This type of CDs might possess aqueous solubility, non-toxicity, excellent stability against photo-bleaching and high visible light activity. In this research, PDs were prepared by a moderate hydrothermal treatment of polyvinyl alcohol, and PDs grafted TiO2 (PDs-TiO2) nanohybrids with TiOC bonds were prepared by a facile in-situ hydrothermal treatment of PDs and Ti (SO4)2. Under visible light irradiation, the PDs-TiO2 demonstrate excellent photocatalytic activity for methyl orange degradation, and the photocatalytic rate constant of PDs-TiO2 is 3.6 and 9.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and commercial P25, respectively. In addition, the PDs-TiO2 exhibit good recycle stability under UV–Vis light irradiation. The interfacial TiOC bonds and the π-conjugated structures in PDs-TiO2 can act as the pathways to quickly transfer the excited electrons between PDs and TiO2, therefore contribute to the excellent photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing the technology of solar energy utilization is favorable for resolving energy crisis and environmental contamination which pose a grave threat to global sustainable development. Hence the search for semiconductor photocatalysts that can harvest the wide spectrum of solar light has become an urgent task (Tian et al., 2015). Titanium dioxide (TiO2), one of the most promising photocatalyst, has been widely used in the fields of air purification, sewage treatment, water splitting, reduction of CO2, and dye sensitized solar cell because of its cheapness, nontoxicity, high photo stability and high photocatalytic activity (Zhao et al., 2010). However, due to the relatively large band gap (3.0–3.2 eV), TiO2 can only absorb ultraviolet (UV) light, which makes up only a small fraction (<4%) of the total solar spectrum (Miao et al., 2016). Therefore, over the past decades, considerable efforts have been made to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation, such as doping with metal and non-metal elements, loading with noble metal particles, coupling with narrow bandgap semiconductors, sensitizing with conjugated dyes (chlorophyll, fluorescein) and so on (Hao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016a). Among these strategies, the modification of TiO2 with carbon nanomaterials has attracted much attention. TiO2/carbon nanocomposites have shown improved optical absorbance in visible spectral range and enhanced photocatalytic activity, when compared with pristine TiO2 (Martins et al., 2016). However, developing a high efficiency and low cost strategy to prepare TiO2 based photocatalysts with visible light response still remains a challenge (Hao et al., 2016).

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a new member of carbon family, have attracted increasing attention due to their superior stability against photo-bleaching, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity and excellent fluorescence emission properties. Since their discovery, CDs have found many applications in the fields of chemical sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis (Yu et al., 2014; Lim et al., 2015). CDs can be prepared from a lot of carbon precursors such as graphene oxide (Hao et al., 2016), graphite rods (Lim et al., 2015), glucose (Tian et al., 2015), ascorbic acid (Miao et al., 2016), candle soot (Ray et al., 2009), polyvinyl alcohol (Zhu et al., 2012), polyacrylamide (Song et al., 2014) and polyethyleneimine (Sun et al., 2013). There are some typical CDs, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polymer dots (PDs) (Song et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2017). GQDs consist of one or a few layers of graphene. CQDs are always spherical, and they possess an obvious crystal lattice (Yu et al., 2016a). PDs are prepared from polymers and usually consist of the carbon core and the connected polymer chains (Zhu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016a).

By physical blending, impregnation, electrophoretic deposition or low temperature hydrothermal methods, some researchers synthesized a wide variety of CDs/TiO2 composites such as GQDs/TiO2 composites (Qu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016b; Yu et al., 2016b; Min et al., 2017) and CQDs/TiO2 composites (Pan et al., 2014; Martins et al., 2016; Miao et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017). These reports have indicated that the CDs/TiO2 composites are good photocatalysts for degradation of pollutants, solar energy conversion and hydrogen evolution, because CDs can harvest visible light and inject excited electrons into the conduction band of TiO2. However, the interfacial interaction between CDs and TiO2 has been seldom discussed in these studies. It is very important to strengthen the interfacial interaction between CDs and TiO2. On one hand, the strong interaction can accelerate the electron transfer on the interface, hence improve the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the strong interaction can prevent CDs from separating and enable the photocatalyst with high stability during photocatalytic degradation (Lei et al., 2014).

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used in the fields of fiber, dispersant, glue and film because of its cheapness and nontoxicity. Under high temperature, PVA can form conjugated structures through dehydration. Recently, Yang et al. (Zhu et al., 2012) synthesized PDs by hydrothermal treatment of PVA. Interestingly, there were a large number of partial degradation of PVA chains radiating out of the carbon core. In our previous works (Lei et al., 2012, 2014), we found that the hydroxyl groups of PVA can form TiOC bonds with TiO2, which can improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports concerning the photocatalytic properties of PDs/TiO2 nanohybrids. Herein, PDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of PVA, then PDs grafted TiO2 (PDs-TiO2) nanohybrids with TiOC bonds were prepared by a facile in-situ hydrothermal treatment of PDs and Ti (SO4)2. The morphology, structure and photocatalytic properties of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids were investigated in detail. A possible mechanism of the improved photocatalytic activity was proposed.

PVA (average polymerization degree of 1750 ± 50) was purchased from Beijing Yili Fine Chemical Co., Ltd, China. Titanium sulfate (Ti (SO4)2, AR) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. Sulfuric acid (AR) was obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. P25 (about 20% rutile and 80% anatase) with a mean diameter of 21 nm was provided by Evonik Industries, Germany. Methyl orange (MO, (CH3)2 NC6H4 NNC6H4 SO3Na), used as the model pollutant, was manufactured by Zhejiang Yongjia Fine Chemical Plant, China. All chemicals were used without further purification.

The PDs were prepared by a moderate hydrothermal treatment of PVA solution (Zhu et al., 2012). 4 g PVA was mixed with 396 mL deionized water in a 500 mL round flask under continuously stirring for 3 h at 95 °C, the obtained PVA solution was colorless and transparent. Then 70 g of the PVA solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated up to 220 °C with a rate of 3 °C/min. After reaction at 220 °C for 6 h, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained PDs solution was yellow and transparent.

The PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids were prepared by in-situ hydrothermal method. Typically, 10 g PDs solution and 2 g Ti (SO4)2 was mixed with 56 g deionized water in a 100 mL Teflon autoclave, and 2 g sulfuric acid was added dropwise into the autoclave under continuously stirring. The mixture was kept stirring for 30 min at room temperature to obtain a transparent solution. Then the autoclave was heated up to 200 °C with a rate of 3 °C/min. After reaction at 200 °C for 6 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The resulting PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids were collected by centrifugation and then washed with deionized water until no SO4 2− was detected by 0.5 mol/L BaCl2 solution. Finally the nanohybrids were dried under vacuum at 100 °C overnight. To investigate the effect of the PDs content on the photocatalytic activity of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids, reactions with different content of PDs solution (5, 10, 15, 20, 25 g) have been done by the same method. The as-prepared samples were labeled as PDs-TiO2-X (X = 5, 10, 15, 20, 25), representing the added amount of PDs solution. TGA was used to evaluate the content of PDs in PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids. The content of PDs in PDs-TiO2-5, PDs-TiO2-10, PDs-TiO2-15, PDs-TiO2-20 and PDs-TiO2-25 samples were 4.1%, 7.7%, 10.9%, 14.4% and 16.1%, respectively. Pure TiO2 was synthesized by the same process in the absence of PDs solution.

Morphology of PDs and PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7700, Hitachi) and a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8020, Hitachi). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was measured using a Malvern 2000 laser particle size instrument equipped with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer equipped with an attenuated total reflectance device (Smart Orbit), the resolution of the spectra was 4 cm−1, and scans were repeated 32 times. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker E500 spectrometer with a high sensitive cylindrical cavity, operating at X-band (9.49 GHz). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out on a Rigaku D/max-2500 diffractometer equipped with Cu Ka radiation at a scan rate of 8°/min. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected on a VG ESCA-Lab220i-XL electron spectrometer with 300 W Mg Ka radiation. The binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8 eV from adventitious carbon. The UV–Vis Diffuse Reflectance spectra were recorded with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). The specific surface area and pore structure were analyzed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen adsorption-desorption method (NOVA, 2000e; Quantachrome), and the pore size distribution was obtained by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method from the desorption branch of the isotherms. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed at room temperature with a F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Autolab Pgsta 302) spectra were carried out in the frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz with an AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV at a bias of 0.23 V (vs Ag/AgCl), the sample solution (2 mg/mL) was mixed with equal volume Nafion solution (5 wt%), and 5 μL mixed solution was dropped on the glassy carbon electrode, the test was carried out after evaporating the solvent at room temperature. The mixture containing 2.5 mM potassium ferricyanide, 2.5 mM potassium ferrocyanide and 0.1 M potassium chloride were used as electrolyte.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by the degradation rate of MO in aqueous solution with an initial concentration of 15 mg/L. In a typical photodegradation experiment, 40 mL MO solution and the sample photocatalyst containing 20 mg TiO2 were placed in a 50 mL beaker. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was magnetically stirred (300 r/min) in dark for 2 h to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between dye and photocatalyst. The light source was a 500 W halogen lamp (Li et al., 2010; Yu et al., 2014, 2016a) equipped with an ultraviolet cut off filter (λ > 420 nm), the average visible light intensity measured by a radiometer was 130 ± 10 mW/cm2, and the average UV–Vis light intensity was 145 ± 10 mW/cm2 without the filter. The lamp was put in a cylindrical glass vessel with a recycling water glass jacket to make sure that the mixed solution was kept at room temperature. At regular times, 5 mL suspension was centrifuged (10 000 r/min, 5 min) and the upper solution was examined by measuring the absorption at 465 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. MO degradation efficiency was calculated by the ratio of concentration (Ct/C0, Ct and C0 can be calculated by the absorbance intensity). For the purpose of comparison, P25, PDs and PDs + TiO2 (physical mixture) samples were also used to degrade MO, respectively.

One unique advantage of carbon dots is their fluorescence emission property. Fig. 1 A shows that the PDs aqueous solution could emit bright cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Fig. S1 shows that the as-prepared PDs can be excited by broad wavelengths and such excitation wavelength-dependent PL behavior has been extensively reported in CDs (Sahu et al., 2012; Lim et al., 2015). The morphology and particle size distribution of PDs were studied by TEM and DLS. TEM images are shown in Fig. 1 (B,C), demonstrating that the prepared PDs are spherical particles with a diameter of less than 5 nm. Combining with the size distribution curve (Fig. S2), the PDs are uniformly dispersed particles with size ranging from 2.7 nm to 5.0 nm, and the average particle size is 3.9 nm. Moreover, according to the HRTEM image (Fig. 1C), the PDs have a degree of crystallinity with average lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) spacing of graphene (Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Yu et al., 2016a). There are noncrystalline regions surrounding the crystalline core, which may correspond to the partial degradation of PVA chains (Zhu et al., 2012), and the thickness of the noncrystalline regions is about 1 nm.

The chemical structures of PDs were confirmed by FTIR, UV–Vis and EPR. Fig. S3 shows FTIR spectra of PDs and PVA, and the strong absorption peaks of PVA at 3276, 2913, 1655, 1429 and 1080 cm−1 are attributed to OH stretching, CH stretching, OH bending, CH2 bending and CO stretching vibration absorption, respectively (Zhang et al., 2013). The absorption peaks of PDs at 1706 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1 are corresponding to CO and CC stretching vibration absorption (Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Miao et al., 2016). Besides, the FTIR comparison shows that PDs retain some absorption peaks of PVA, implying that PDs possess a significant amount of partial degradation of PVA chains radiating out of the carbon core (Zhu et al., 2012). UV–Vis spectra are shown in Fig. S4A, and there is no obvious absorption peak in the spectrum of PVA. The spectrum of PDs indicates typical absorption peaks at 227, 278 and 330 nm, which are identified as aromatic CC, CO and conjugated CC bonds (Bozetine et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016a, 2016b, 2016c), respectively. And these absorption peaks are speculated to the formation of graphitic structure, carboxyl groups and conjugated chains (Zhu et al., 2012; Bozetine et al., 2016). EPR is a widely used probe method to detect the unpaired electrons in π-conjugated materials (Wang et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2014). In Fig. S4B, PDs appear a distinct signal at g = 2.004 which is attributed to the delocalized π-π* electrons of the conjugated carbon chains (Lei et al., 2014; Di et al., 2016), and PVA gives no response. These results confirm the formation of π-conjugated structures in PDs. These π-conjugated structures in PDs would be excited under visible light irradiation, so PDs have great potential in increasing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

According to the above results, the structure of PDs could be deduced. As shown in Scheme S1, PDs can be prepared by a moderate hydrothermal treatment of PVA, interestingly, there are a large number of partial degradation of PVA chains radiating out of the carbon core. The carbon cores of PDs have the graphite-like structure, because the obvious crystal lattices were observed. There are a large number of hydroxyl, carboxyl, CC and conjugated CC bonds on the surface of PDs, and this hydroxyl and carboxyl groups providing the possibility of grafting PDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles.

Fig. S5 shows XRD patterns of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids. Compared with the standard card of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No.84-1285), all the diffraction peaks of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2 can be readily indexed to the standard anatase TiO2. The average crystallite sizes were calculated by Scherrer's equation based on the full width at half maximum of (101) crystal planes, and the obtained sizes are 18.3, 16.8, 16.4, 18.3, 17.9 and 17.1 nm for pure TiO2, PDs-TiO2-5, PDs-TiO2-10, PDs-TiO2-15, PDs-TiO2-20 and PDs-TiO2-25, respectively. So PDs had little effect on the crystallite size of TiO2 during the in-situ hydrothermal synthesis process.

The morphology and structure of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 were observed by SEM and TEM. Fig. S6 (A, B) shows that the particle size distribution of TiO2 is not uniform, and lots of small particles and large aggregates are mixed together. From a single magnified aggregate, it can be seen that the aggregates are built by uniform TiO2 nanoparticles. Fig. S6 (C, D) demonstrate that the PDs-TiO2-10 is composed of many porous aggregates with the size of 0.5–2 μm. It is interesting to find that almost the porous aggregates can be separated from water in 1 h without stirring (Fig. S7), suggesting that PDs-TiO2-10 is easy to recycle by gravitational force. Furthermore, the elemental mapping images of PDs-TiO2-10 (Fig. S8) obtained by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer suggest that Ti (red), C (green), O (blue) elements are distributed throughout the entire sample. TEM was further used to observe the distribution of PDs in PDs-TiO2-10. As shown in Fig. 2 (A, B), pure TiO2 particles have an irregular shape with a smooth surface, and the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm agrees well with the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 (Su et al., 2016). The TEM images of PDs-TiO2-10 are shown in Fig. 2 (C, D), revealing that a great many of black dots are attached on the surface of TiO2, and the average size of these black dots is about 3.9 nm, which is consistent with the size of PDs.

Fig. S9 shows N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distribution curves of the as-prepared TiO2 (A) and PDs-TiO2-10 (B). Isotherms of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 can be classified as type IV in the IUPAC classification with distinct hysteresis loops observed, which is characteristic of porous materials, indicating the presence of mesopores in samples. And the mesopores in TiO2 reflect the interstices in TiO2 aggregates (Fig. S6), because there are no mesopores in TiO2 single nanocrystal (Fig. 2). The narrow pore size distributions were found to be 16.3 nm for TiO2 and 6.6 nm for PDs-TiO2-10, meaning that PDs formed smaller pores in PDs-TiO2-10. Multilevel structure and accumulation form probably caused the lower pore size in PDs-TiO2-10. The specific surface areas of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 were calculated by BET method and values of 73.9 and 79.2 m2/g, respectively, were obtained. It's reported that the specific surface area of P25 is about 50 m2/g (Martins et al., 2016). The above results suggest that pure TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 may provide more active sites for the adsorption and degradation of organic molecules than P25.

FTIR and XPS were used to study the interaction between TiO2 and PDs. Fig. 3 A shows the FTIR spectra. For TiO2, the peak at 3210 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching and bending vibration of OH bands were observed, which results from the physically adsorbed water and surface hydroxyl groups (Cheng et al., 2015). Compared with TiO2, several new peaks appearing at 2923, 1710 and 1442 cm−1 in PDs-TiO2-10 are attributed to CH stretching, CO stretching and CH2 bending vibration absorption respectively, suggesting that PDs are absorbed on the surface of TiO2. In particular, the very weak peak at 1263 cm−1 may be ascribed to the TiOC bonds vibration absorption, indicating that PDs are chemically grafted on the surface of TiO2 (Min et al., 2008; Lei et al., 2014; Fujisawa et al., 2016). This results were further confirmed by XPS. The full-scale XPS spectrum of PDs-TiO2-10 (Fig. S10A) shows the existence of Ti, O, and C elements, suggesting the presence of PDs in PDs-TiO2-10. High resolution C1s XPS spectrum of PDs-TiO2-10 (Fig. S10B) shows four main peaks at 284.8, 285.9, 286.6 and 289.2 eV attributed to CC/CC, TiOC, CO and OCO bonds respectively (Cong et al., 2011; Lei et al., 2014; Martins et al., 2016; Su et al., 2016). In addition, the high resolution Ti2p XPS spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 were also collected (Fig. 3B). The peaks at 464.8 and 459.1 eV observed in PDs-TiO2-10 for Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 are slightly shifted towards a higher binding energy compared with those in pure TiO2. These results suggest that the chemical environment of Ti in PDs-TiO2-10 has been changed due to the formation of the TiOC bonds (Liu et al., 2012; Vijayan et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2014).

Photoabsorption is one of the key factors affecting the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 4 A, pure TiO2 has almost no absorption in the visible light region, while the PDs-TiO2-10 shows an obvious absorption ranging from 400 to 800 nm. The inset photographs show that the color of pure TiO2 is white and the color of PDs-TiO2-10 is brown. In addition, the band gap energies of pure TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 (Fig. 4B) calculated by the transformed Kubelka-Munk function are 3.05 and 2.71 eV, respectively. The obvious decrease of the bandgap of PDs-TiO2-10 may be attributed to the chemical bonding between TiO2 and PDs with the formation of TiOC bonds (Martins et al., 2016), and this could explain the enhanced photocatalytic activity of PDs-TiO2-10 under visible light irradiation.

Fig. 5 A shows the comparison of photocatalytic efficiency between PDs-TiO2-10 and some control samples. PDs + TiO2 represents the physical mixture of PDs and TiO2, and the content of PDs is 7.7%. Under visible light irradiation, MO is very stable in aqueous solution, revealing almost unchanged concentration. After adding PDs, the concentration of MO is also unchanged, indicating that PDs have no visible light photocatalytic activity (Qu et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2015). The PDs + TiO2 sample is much less active, few inferior to the pure TiO2. It is obvious that PDs-TiO2-10 shows the highest photocatalytic activity in all samples. The marked difference between PDs-TiO2-10 and PDs + TiO2 indicates that the interfacial TiOC bonds in PDs-TiO2-10 are beneficial to improve the photocatalytic activity. Fig. S11 shows the first-order linear fitting lines of MO degradation kinetic curves under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic rate constant k of PDs-TiO2-10 is 0.161 h−1 which is 3.6 and 9.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2 (0.045 h−1) and P25 (0.017 h−1), respectively.

For practical application, photocatalyst is always expected to be useful for a relatively long time under UV–Vis light irradiation (Liu et al., 2015; Miao et al., 2016). So the photocatalytic activity and cycle-use stability of PDs-TiO2-10 was measured under UV–Vis light irradiation. Fig. 5B shows photocatalytic degradation of MO solution under UV–Vis light irradiation. For PDs-TiO2-10, the degradation efficiency of MO is 96.7% after irradiation for 8 h. In contrast, the degradation efficiency of MO is 79.8% and 76.6% for TiO2 and P25, respectively. The photocatalytic rate constant k of PDs-TiO2-10 (0.286 h−1) is 1.8 and 2.0 times higher than that of TiO2 (0.163 h−1) and P25 (0.145 h−1), respectively. The marked difference between PDs-TiO2-10 and PDs + TiO2 indicates that the interfacial TiOC bonds are beneficial to improve the photocatalytic activity under UV–Vis light irradiation. Fig. 5C shows the recycle photocatalytic performance test of PDs-TiO2-10 under UV–Vis light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of MO is 96.7% in the first cycle while the efficiency is 89.5% in the fifth cycle (after 40 h), so the PDs-TiO2-10 exhibits good cycle-use stability under UV–Vis light irradiation.

To further explore the photocatalytic activity of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids, the effect of grafting amount of PDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5D, the grafting amount of PDs in the present system can largely affect the MO degradation efficiency. After the grafting of TiO2 with PDs, the photocatalytic rate constant increases gradually with the increasing of PDs content. The highest photocatalytic rate constant was observed for PDs-TiO2-10. However, further increasing the grafting amount of PDs results in the gradual decrease of the photocatalytic rate constant, which is probably due to the decreasing of the light absorption intensity of TiO2 by the opacity and light scattering of the PDs, and the reduced photocatalytic active sites (Xiang et al., 2011; Yu et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between the structure and photocatalytic activity of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the photocatalytic mechanism of PDs-TiO2 is proposed in Scheme 1 . There are a large number of hydroxyl, carboxyl, CC and conjugated CC bonds on the surface of PDs. Under high temperature and high pressure, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of PDs can react with the surface hydroxyl groups of TiO2 to form TiOC bonds. The special chemical structures of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids play crucial roles for the improved photocatalytic activity. Firstly, the carbon core and conjugated CC bonds in PDs can be excited by visible light irradiation. Then, the excited electrons are injected to the conduction band of TiO2 through the pathways of π-conjugated structures and TiOC bonds. The injected electrons subsequently migrate to the edge of TiO2 and react with the oxygen adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 to generate active oxygen species (*O2 −) (Lei et al., 2014; Miao et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the intermediate H2O2 species and hydroxyl radicals (*OH) are produced by the reactions between *O2 − and water (Ebrahimi et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2016). *O2 − and *OH are extremely strong oxidant for the partial or complete degradation of organic chemicals (Qu et al., 2013; Su et al., 2016). It is reported that CH3, SO3 − and NN groups in methyl orange are destroyed by *O2 − or *OH firstly, and then the intermediates such as demethylated species, hydroxylated species and phenylsulfonic radicals are further degraded into NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO2, H2O et al. (Baiocchi et al., 2002; Bianco Prevot et al., 2004; Li et al., 2008).

In addition, under UV light irradiation, PDs can serve as electron reservoirs to trap photogenerated electrons by TiO2 and ensure fast charge transportation in view of its interfacial TiOC bonds and π-conjugated structures, and therefore, an effective charge separation can be achieved (Zhang et al., 2010; Xie et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2014).

To further confirm the above photocatalytic mechanism of PDs-TiO2, we investigated the charge separation and transfer from PDs to TiO2. EIS is applied as an additional characterization tool to distinguish the interfacial structure of the modified nanoparticles (Li et al., 2011). The semicircle portion in EIS spectrum represents the electron transfer limited process, and the semicircle diameter equals the electron transfer resistance (Liu et al., 2012). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the typical electrochemical impedance spectra were presented as Nyquist plots. After being grafted by PDs, the semicircle in the plot became shorter, indicating a decrease in the charge transfer resistance on the surface of TiO2 (Zhang et al., 2010). PL emission spectra have been widely used to measure the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs in semiconductor particles (Caudillo-Flores et al., 2016). The lower PL intensity indicates the lower recombination rate (Li et al., 2013a). The PL spectra of TiO2 and PDs-TiO2-10 excitated by UV light are presented in Fig. 6B. The peak appeared at about 402 nm is ascribed to the emission of the band gap transition in anatase TiO2, and the peak around 470 nm is emission signal originating from the charge transfer transition of oxygen vacancies trapped electrons (Li et al., 2013b; Qu et al., 2016). Notably, the PL intensity of TiO2 decreases obviously after being grafted by PDs, indicating that the recombination rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs is inhibited greatly in PDs-TiO2-10, which can be attributed to the effective charge transfer between TiO2 and PDs. Therefore, the above photocatalytic mechanism of PDs-TiO2 is confirmed.

In summary, we have developed a facile and green method to prepare chemically bonded PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids by in-situ hydrothermal treatment of PDs and Ti (SO4)2. The photocatalytic rate constant of PDs-TiO2 is 3.6 and 9.5 times higher than that of TiO2 and commercial P25 under visible light irradiation, respectively. And the PDs-TiO2 exhibits good recycle stability under UV–Vis light irradiation. This is attributed to both the effective visible light harvest by PDs and the efficient electron transfer through π-conjugated structures and TiOC bonds between TiO2 and PDs. This novel design of PDs-TiO2 nanohybrids may open up a new avenue for fabricating high performance visible light photocatalysts and facilitating their practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 51403217 and 21504003]; the "Strategic Priority Research Program" of the Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. XDA09030200] and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS [Grant No. 2017041].

ce:section.ce:para:
